race.name: Cycle to the Sun
race.tagline: Sea level to 10,023 feet. 36 miles of continuous climbing up a Hawaiian volcano. The House of the Sun awaits.

vitals.date_specific: 2025: August 2. 2026: TBD (registration opens March 1 on BikeReg)
terrain_types: Continuous uphill — sea level to 10,023 ft summit
Multiple climate zones: tropical, temperate, alpine, volcanic
vitals.registration: Online via bikereg.com. Opens March 1. Limited to ~200 entries due to Haleakala National Park permit. Cost: $175 (residents) / $200 (non-residents)
vitals.prize_purse: Prizes for overall male/female/relay winners and age group category winners
vitals.aid_stations: 3 aid stations: ~3,000 ft (near Kula Lodge), ~6,500 ft (Haleakala National Park entrance), ~8,000 ft
vitals.cutoff_time: No published cutoff. Self-paced. Most riders finish in 2.5-5 hours.
distance_options: Full climb: 36 miles, 10,023 ft — Pa'ia to Haleakala summit
Three-person relay: changeover at ~2,700 ft and ~6,500 ft

climate.primary: Tropical to volcanic alpine — extreme vertical temperature gradient
climate.description: The climb traverses every climate zone Hawaii has to offer in a single ride. Sea-level Pa'ia starts warm and humid (75-85F) with tropical vegetation. By 4,000 feet, eucalyptus groves and cooling temperatures (65-70F). Above the treeline at ~6,500 feet, the landscape turns barren and volcanic. The summit at 10,023 feet can be 40-50F with fierce trade winds and wind chill well below that. You ride from the tropics to conditions that feel sub-arctic in 36 miles.
challenges: 40-degree Fahrenheit temperature drop from start to summit
Trade winds intensify above 6,000 ft — sustained 15-25 mph headwinds common
Summit wind chill can feel below freezing even in summer
Intense UV exposure at altitude — tropical sun plus thin atmosphere
Humidity and heat at lower elevations cause early sweat loss

terrain.surface: Well-maintained paved road throughout (Haleakala Highway / Crater Road). Smooth tarmac from Pa'ia through Makawao, Kula, and into Haleakala National Park to the summit.
features: Continuous uphill from sea level to 10,023 ft — no meaningful descents
Average gradient ~5% with sustained sections of 8-10%
Short steep kick of ~10% through Makawao town
Final summit approach at 10% gradient in thin air above 9,500 ft
The 'ribbon road' — exposed switchbacks above the cloud line through volcanic moonscape

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: One of the world's great single climbs — sea level to 10,023 ft on a Hawaiian volcano. T2 by score (74). p4 cannot promote into T1.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: Elevation=5 and altitude=5 for 10,023 ft of continuous climbing to genuine high altitude. Adventure=5 for tropical-to-volcanic climate traverse on a Pacific island. Experience=5 for the transformative sea-to-summit journey. Technicality=1 (all paved). Expenses=2 (Hawaii is expensive). cultural_impact=4 for Hawaiian cultural significance and iconic American climbing challenge.

logistics.explanation: Getting to Maui is the first challenge — flights from the mainland to Kahului (OGG), then a rental car, then navigating a small Hawaiian island. Pa'ia is a charming surf town, not a cycling hub. The event is well-organized by Go Cycling Maui under Donnie Arnoult, a former Cat 1 pro, and has been running smoothly since 2008. Three aid stations along the climb are adequate. But the ~200-rider cap imposed by the National Park permit means this is a small, intimate event — not a mass-start machine. The logistics of getting yourself and a bike to a Hawaiian island for a single 36-mile uphill race require commitment.

length.explanation: 36 miles sounds short until you realize every single mile goes uphill. There is no flat. There is no descent. It is 36 miles of continuous climbing from sea level to 10,023 feet. The fastest riders finish in under 2.5 hours; most take 3-5 hours. It's not a century, but the relentless upward gradient and altitude accumulation make those 36 miles feel much longer. The relay option (3-person teams with changeovers at ~2,700 ft and ~6,500 ft) acknowledges the difficulty.

technicality.explanation: Paved road the entire way. Haleakala Highway and Crater Road are smooth, well-maintained Hawaiian state roads. There are no gravel sections, no technical descents (you go up and only up), and the bike handling demands are minimal. The challenge is entirely physiological — sustained climbing power, altitude tolerance, and the mental fortitude to keep pedaling upward for hours. A road bike is the only sensible choice.

elevation.explanation: 10,023 feet of continuous elevation gain in a single climb. This is the fifth-highest continuous paved cycling climb on Earth by elevation gained, behind only Mauna Kea, Mauna Loa, Death Road Bolivia, and Wuling Pass Taiwan. Unlike multi-pass events where you descend between climbs, every foot of elevation here is earned in one relentless push. From sea level to summit, there is no respite. On the PJAMM Cycling database, Haleakala ranks #2 in the United States and #30 in the world.

climate.explanation: You ride through every climate zone Hawaii possesses in a single effort. Tropical humidity at sea level. Eucalyptus-cooled temperate forest at 4,000 feet. Barren volcanic scrubland above the treeline. And a summit that regularly drops to 40F with fierce trade winds. The 40-degree temperature drop demands clothing strategy — you need arm warmers and a vest you can deploy above 7,000 feet, but you'll overheat if you start with them at sea level. The UV at altitude is fierce. Summer trade winds above 6,000 feet can be savage headwinds.

altitude.explanation: The summit of Haleakala sits at 10,023 feet — higher than any pass in the Alps, higher than any summit in the Pyrenees. At this altitude, oxygen availability is roughly 70% of sea level. The final push above 9,000 feet, with gradients hitting 10% in thin air, is where the ride becomes genuinely difficult. Unlike European mountain passes where you descend after the summit, here you climb into the thin air and stay there. Riders from sea level will feel this acutely from about 7,000 feet onward. The combination of sustained effort and altitude is the race's defining challenge.

adventure.explanation: You start on a North Shore surf town on a tropical island in the middle of the Pacific Ocean. You finish above the clouds inside a volcanic crater at 10,023 feet. Along the way, you ride through sugarcane fields, tropical rainforest, eucalyptus groves, pine forests, alpine scrub, and finally barren volcanic moonscape — all in 36 miles. The views from above the cloud line are staggering: the Pacific Ocean stretching to infinity, Maui's central valley below, and the summit crater of a dormant volcano ahead. Haleakala means 'House of the Sun' in Hawaiian. You ride to the place where the demigod Maui is said to have lassoed the sun. There is no cycling experience remotely like it.

prestige.explanation: Cycling to the summit of Haleakala is on the bucket list of every serious climber who has heard of it. The course record was held by Ryder Hesjedal (2:32:51, 2009) until Mike Woods broke it (2:32:24, 2013) — both pro WorldTour riders. The event itself has run since the 1980s, was revived in 2001, and has been consistently organized since 2008. It's not the global prestige of La Marmotte or Alpe d'Huez, but within US cycling, riding Haleakala carries serious credibility. It's the American equivalent of riding Ventoux — a single iconic mountain that defines a region.

race_quality.explanation: Solid grassroots organization. Donnie Arnoult and Go Cycling Maui have run this event reliably since 2008. Three aid stations, age-group categories (18 & Under through 70+), and prizes for overall and category winners give it competitive structure. Entry fees fund the Pa'ia Youth and Cultural Center, which gives the event genuine community purpose. But this is a 200-rider island hill climb, not a 7,000-rider European cyclosportive. The intimacy is part of the charm, but the infrastructure reflects the scale.

experience.explanation: The Cycle to the Sun is one of the most singular experiences in cycling. You leave a Hawaiian beach town at dawn, climb through the tropics, burst through the cloud layer, and arrive at the summit of a 10,023-foot volcano where the temperature has dropped 40 degrees and the Pacific Ocean stretches to the horizon in every direction. The landscape above the treeline is lunar — raw volcanic rock and silence. The progression from humid tropical air to thin, cold alpine atmosphere is physiologically dramatic and emotionally overwhelming. When you stand at the summit crater of Haleakala, you've ridden from the bottom of the ocean (functionally) to the top of a volcano. It is, quite literally, cycling from the sea to the sun.

community.explanation: With ~200 riders, this is an intimate event. The Pa'ia cycling community is tight and welcoming. Entry fees benefit the Pa'ia Youth and Cultural Center, giving the ride genuine charitable purpose. The small field means you'll know the people around you by the end. But the format — a lone uphill grind — doesn't naturally foster the shoulder-to-shoulder camaraderie of peloton-style events. Many riders are visiting from the mainland, creating a transient community rather than a deeply rooted local one.

field_depth.explanation: 200 riders is a small field by any standard. The competitive depth is limited — this is a bucket-list challenge more than a race. The course records held by Hesjedal and Woods were set outside the organized event during personal training efforts. The age-group categories provide competitive structure, but the field lacks the depth of major mainland events. Serious climbers enter for the personal challenge, not the competition.

value.explanation: $175-200 for a single 36-mile uphill ride might seem steep, but the entry supports the Pa'ia Youth and Cultural Center, covers National Park permits, and funds three aid stations on a volcanic mountain. For a one-day event in Hawaii with genuine charitable impact, it's reasonable. The value is in the experience — you can't put a price on riding to the top of Haleakala.

expenses.explanation: Hawaii is Hawaii. Round-trip flights from the mainland run $400-800+. Maui accommodation in summer ranges from $200-500+/night. A rental car is essential — there's no alternative on Maui. You'll want 3-4 nights minimum: arrive, acclimatize, race, recover. Total budget from the mainland: $1,500-3,000+ easily. If you're already on Maui, the entry fee is the only major cost. If you're flying in specifically for this race, it's a significant investment.

biased_opinion.summary: Cycle to the Sun is not the hardest race in the world, or the most prestigious, or the most competitive. It is, however, one of the most extraordinary cycling experiences on the planet. You start at sea level on a tropical island and ride continuously uphill for 36 miles until you're standing at 10,023 feet on the rim of a volcanic crater, above the clouds, in 40-degree air that was 80 degrees when you started. You traverse every climate zone Hawaii has to offer — tropical, temperate, alpine, volcanic — in a single effort. The name Haleakala means 'House of the Sun,' and when you reach the summit, you understand why. This is not a race you enter to win. This is a ride you do to remind yourself that cycling can take you to places that feel like the edge of the Earth.
strengths: 10,023 feet of continuous climbing from sea level — the fifth-highest continuous paved cycling climb on Earth
Tropical-to-volcanic climate traverse: four distinct climate zones in 36 miles
Above-the-clouds summit views from a volcanic crater at 10,023 feet
Deep cultural significance: Haleakala, the 'House of the Sun' in Hawaiian mythology
weaknesses: Hawaii logistics: flights, accommodation, and rental car make this an expensive undertaking
Small field (~200 riders) limits competitive depth and event atmosphere
36 miles of relentless uphill — no descents, no flat, no variety in effort type
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you've ever wanted to ride from the ocean to the sky, Haleakala is the only place on Earth that delivers exactly that. The sun is waiting.

final_verdict.one_liner: Sea level to 10,023 feet. One climb. One volcano. The House of the Sun.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want one of cycling's most extraordinary single-climb experiences. Yes if riding through four climate zones to the summit of a Hawaiian volcano sounds like a lifetime memory. Skip if you need competitive depth or can't justify Hawaii travel costs for a single 36-mile uphill ride.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want massive US climbing with more distance: try Death Ride — Tour of the California Alps. If you want the definitive European mountain sportive: try La Marmotte. If you want US high-altitude climbing with a race atmosphere: try Crusher in the Tushar.

logistics.airport: Kahului (OGG) — Maui's only commercial airport. 20 minutes to Pa'ia. Direct flights from most West Coast cities and connecting flights from major mainland hubs.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Pa'ia for the closest access to the start. Pa'ia Inn and nearby vacation rentals are walking distance. Kahului hotels (Hampton Inn, Courtyard Marriott) are 20 minutes away and cheaper. Book early — Maui summer accommodation fills fast and prices are high. A rental car is non-negotiable on Maui.
logistics.food: Pa'ia has excellent restaurants: Flatbread Company, Cafe des Amis, Pa'ia Fish Market. Carb-load Hawaiian-style — rice, poke, plate lunches. Post-ride recovery at Pa'ia's surf-town cafes. Pack nutrition carefully for the climb — 3 aid stations but 36 miles of uphill demands self-sufficiency.
logistics.packet_pickup: Pre-race in Pa'ia area. Details on cycletothesun.com.
logistics.parking: Pa'ia town. Arrive early — street parking is limited in this small surf town.

history.founder: Originally held in the 1980s; revived by Kalima O Maui in 2001; organized by Go Cycling Maui / Donnie Arnoult since 2008
history.origin_story: The Cycle to the Sun was first held in the 1980s as a way to challenge cyclists against the ultimate Hawaiian climb — sea level to the summit of Haleakala volcano at 10,023 feet. The event languished for years before Kalima O Maui acquired the rights and revived it in 2001. Since 2008, Donnie Arnoult — a former Cat 1 pro racer and owner of Maui Cyclery and Go Cycling Maui in Pa'ia — has organized and produced the event, turning it into Maui's premier competitive cycling challenge. Entry fees benefit the Pa'ia Youth and Cultural Center.
notable_moments: 2001: Kalima O Maui revives the race after years of dormancy
2008: Donnie Arnoult and Go Cycling Maui take over organization
2009: Ryder Hesjedal sets Haleakala climbing record of 2:32:51 during personal training
2013: Mike Woods breaks the record with 2:32:24 — two WorldTour pros validating the climb's legendary status
Event benefits Pa'ia Youth and Cultural Center annually
history.reputation: The American cycling pilgrimage. One of the world's great single climbs. The only place on Earth where you ride from a tropical beach to above the clouds on a volcanic summit in a single continuous effort.

course_description.character: Pure uphill. There is no flat. There is no descent. The road leaves Pa'ia at sea level and climbs continuously for 36 miles through four distinct climate zones to the summit of Haleakala at 10,023 feet. The average gradient is 5%, but sections through Makawao hit 10% and the final summit approach reaches 10-18% in oxygen-depleted air. The landscape transforms completely as you climb: tropical vegetation gives way to eucalyptus, then pine, then barren volcanic rock above the treeline. You will ride above the clouds. You will arrive at the rim of a volcanic crater. Every foot of elevation is earned.

suffering_zones.label: Pa'ia to Makawao — The Tropical Start
suffering_zones.desc: Leave Pa'ia at sea level in warm, humid tropical air. The road rises gently through sugarcane fields and residential areas before steepening into Makawao town at ~1,500 feet. A short 10% kick through Makawao wakes up the legs. Lush tropical vegetation surrounds you. The humidity is real — drink early, drink often.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Sea level to ~1,500 ft, tropical vegetation, 3-5% average with 10% kick through Makawao

suffering_zones.label: Makawao to Kula — The Temperate Transition
suffering_zones.desc: Above Makawao, the road enters upcountry Maui. Eucalyptus groves and ranch country replace the tropical lowlands. Temperatures begin to cool noticeably. The gradient is steady at 5-6%. The first aid station appears near Kula Lodge at ~3,000 feet. You can see the ocean behind you. The summit is still impossibly far above.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: ~1,500 to ~3,000 ft, eucalyptus and ranch country, steady 5-6%

suffering_zones.label: Kula to Park Entrance — Above the Treeline
suffering_zones.desc: The road enters Haleakala National Park around 6,500 feet. The second aid station marks the transition. Vegetation thins dramatically — pine forests give way to scrub, then bare volcanic rock. The air is noticeably thinner. Trade winds can be fierce at this elevation, creating sustained headwinds of 15-25 mph. Temperatures drop below 60F. You begin to see the cloud layer forming below you.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: ~3,000 to ~6,500 ft, vegetation transition, trade wind exposure, 5-8% gradient

suffering_zones.label: Park Entrance to Summit — The House of the Sun
suffering_zones.desc: The final 8 miles above the park entrance are the ride's defining challenge. You are above the cloud line. The landscape is lunar — raw volcanic cinder, no vegetation, nothing between you and the Pacific sky. The air at 8,000-10,000 feet contains roughly 70% of sea-level oxygen. Gradients hit 8-10% with spikes to 18%. The final summit approach, with its exposed switchbacks through the volcanic crater rim, demands everything you have left. Temperature: 40-50F with wind chill potentially below freezing. When you reach the summit at 10,023 feet and look into the crater of Haleakala, you've arrived at the House of the Sun.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: ~6,500 to 10,023 ft, volcanic moonscape, 8-18% gradients, severe altitude effects, wind chill

course_description.signature_challenge: The final 8 miles above the park entrance. Above the cloud line, through barren volcanic landscape, with 70% oxygen and gradients hitting 18%, you grind to the summit crater of Haleakala at 10,023 feet. It is the closest thing to riding on another planet.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved Hawaiian volcanic mountain road — continuous uphill
guide_variables.race_weather: Extreme vertical temperature gradient: 80F at sea level to 40F at summit with trade winds
race_challenges: 10,023 feet of continuous climbing from sea level — no descents, no flat
Altitude effects above 7,000 ft — oxygen reduced to ~70% of sea level at summit
40-degree Fahrenheit temperature drop from start to finish
Trade wind headwinds above 6,000 ft — sustained 15-25 mph

non_negotiables.requirement: Sustained climbing endurance — 3-5 hour continuous uphill efforts in training
non_negotiables.why: There is no flat and no descent. Your legs must sustain 36 miles of continuous climbing output. Train with the longest sustained climbs you can find, supplemented by indoor trainer sessions at steady threshold effort.

non_negotiables.requirement: Altitude awareness and preparation for riding above 8,000 ft
non_negotiables.why: The summit is at 10,023 ft where oxygen is ~70% of sea level. Sea-level riders will feel significant performance degradation from ~7,000 ft onward. If possible, arrive on Maui 2-3 days early. Consider altitude simulation training or hypoxic preparation.

non_negotiables.requirement: Layering strategy tested and rehearsed for 40-degree temperature swing
non_negotiables.why: You start in 80F tropical humidity and finish in 40F volcanic wind. Arm warmers, a wind vest, and full-finger gloves must be accessible mid-climb. Practice deploying layers at tempo effort — fumbling at 9,000 feet with numb fingers is not optional.
